title: Floating-Point Numbers
summary: Represent real numbers using fixed-point, BCD, and IEEE 754 floating-point formats.
content_html:
The Challenge of Real Numbers

Real numbers can be infinitely precise (pi = 3.14159..., 1/3 = 0.333...), but computers have finite memory. The representation we choose impacts both value range and precision.

Fixed-Point Representation

Dedicate a fixed number of bits for the integer part and fractional part. The binary point stays at a fixed location.

Bit weights: 16  8  4  2  1 . 0.5  0.25  0.125
Bits:         0  1  0  1  0 .  1    0     1
Value: 8 + 2 + 0.5 + 0.125 = 10.625

Fixed-Point Steps

  1. Convert the integer part to binary
  2. Place the binary point
  3. Convert the fractional part (multiply by 2 repeatedly)
Represent 25.6640625 in fixed format:
  25 = 16+8+1 = 000011001
  0.6640625 = 0.5+0.125+0.03125+0.0078125 = .1010101
  Result: 000011001.1010101

Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD)

Each decimal digit stored as a 4-bit nibble. Simple to convert but wastes ~37% of storage.

BCD example: 7926.34
  7    9    2    6  .  3    4
 0111 1001 0010 0110  0011 0100

Floating-Point Representation

Fixed-point wastes bits for very small or very large numbers. Floating-point uses variable allocation, like scientific notation.

-273.5 = -1.000100011 x 2^8

IEEE 754 Standard (32-bit Single Precision)

Bit 31  |  Bits 30-23  |  Bits 22-0
  Sign  |   Exponent   |  Mantissa
  1 bit |    8 bits    |   23 bits

Value = (-1)^sign x 1.mantissa x 2^(exponent - 127)

The leading 1 before the binary point is implicit (always 1, not stored).

Converting to IEEE 754

Example: -273.5 as 32-bit IEEE 754
1. Sign bit = 1 (negative)
2. 273.5 in binary = 100010001.1
3. Normalise: 1.000100011 x 2^8 (shifted 8 places)
4. Mantissa = 00010001100000000000000
5. Exponent = 8 + 127 = 135 = 10000111
6. Result: 1 10000111 00010001100000000000000

Special Values

  • +Infinity: exponent = all 1s, mantissa = 0
  • -Infinity: sign=1, exponent = all 1s, mantissa = 0
  • NaN: exponent = all 1s, mantissa not 0
  • Zero: all bits = 0

Floating-Point Hardware

  • FPU (Floating-Point Unit): dedicated processor for FP operations
  • Most modern CPUs contain FPUs
  • Simple microcontrollers may emulate FP in software

Limitations

  • Not all real numbers can be represented exactly (0.1 is repeating in binary)
  • Rounding errors accumulate in long calculations
  • More bits = more precision but slower operations

exercises.title: IEEE 754 sign bit
exercises.prompt: In IEEE 754 single precision, how many bits are used for the sign?

exercises.title: Exponent bits
exercises.prompt: How many bits are in the exponent field of IEEE 754 single precision?

exercises.title: Mantissa bits
exercises.prompt: How many bits are in the mantissa of IEEE 754 single precision?

exercises.title: Exponent bias
exercises.prompt: The exponent bias in IEEE 754 single precision is:

exercises.title: NaN
options: Exponent is all 1s and mantissa is non-zero
exercises.answer: Exponent is all 1s and mantissa is non-zero

exercises.title: Fixed-point limitation
exercises.prompt: What is the main drawback of fixed-point representation compared to floating-point?
options: Wastes bits for very large or very small numbers
exercises.answer: Wastes bits for very large or very small numbers

exercises.title: BCD storage overhead
exercises.prompt: Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD) wastes approximately what percentage of storage compared to fixed-point?

exercises.title: Implicit bit
exercises.prompt: In IEEE 754, why is the leading 1 of the mantissa not stored?
options: It is always 1 in normalised form, so storing it wastes a bit
It is included in the exponent
exercises.answer: It is always 1 in normalised form, so storing it wastes a bit

exercises.title: Representation limits
exercises.prompt: Why can 0.1 not be stored exactly in IEEE 754 floating-point?
options: 0.1 is a repeating fraction in binary
exercises.answer: 0.1 is a repeating fraction in binary
